checks.scenario: A team builds a voice agent with a target of under 500ms end-to-end latency. In testing they measure a mean latency of 380ms and declare success. After deploying to 100 users, support tickets flood in about the agent feeling sluggish and unresponsive.
checks.question: What measurement mistake did the team make, and what should they measure instead?
options: A. They should have measured median (P50) instead of mean latency
B. They should have measured P95 latency rather than mean; users experience tail latency, and a P95 of 1,200ms with a mean of 380ms is common when there are occasional slow LLM or TTS responses
C. 500ms is too aggressive a target; they should have set 1,000ms as the goal
D. They should have measured throughput (requests per second) rather than latency
checks.explanation: Mean latency hides the distribution. A voice agent can have a 380ms mean while 5% of turns take 1,200ms+ due to LLM token generation variance, API queue wait, or network jitter. Those 5% slow turns are what users notice and complain about. The latency target for a voice agent should be expressed as P95 (95th percentile), not mean. A mean of 380ms with P95 of 1,200ms fails the user experience requirement even though the mean looks good.

checks.scenario: A voice agent pipeline waits for the complete LLM response before sending any text to the TTS API. The LLM generates a 3-sentence response in 800ms. The TTS synthesizes and starts playing at 1,200ms after the user finishes speaking.
checks.question: How does streaming TTS reduce this latency, and what is the mechanism?
options: A. Streaming TTS uses a smaller TTS model that generates audio faster

B. Streaming TTS sends each complete sentence to TTS as it is generated by the LLM, so the first sentence is being synthesized while the LLM is still generating sentences 2 and 3 - reducing first-audio time by 200-400ms
C. Streaming TTS pre-caches common responses so synthesis is instant
D. Streaming TTS reduces LLM generation time by sending partial prompts
checks.explanation: The streaming TTS pattern uses a SentenceBuffer to detect sentence boundaries in the LLM token stream. When a complete sentence is detected (ending with '.', '!', '?'), it is sent to TTS immediately without waiting for the rest of the response. TTS begins synthesizing sentence 1 while the LLM is still generating sentences 2 and 3. The user hears the first audio chunk 200-400ms earlier than in the batch pattern where the full response is assembled first.

checks.scenario: A voice agent is deployed for customer service calls. Users frequently interrupt the agent mid-sentence to correct it or ask a different question. The current implementation ignores these interruptions and the agent continues speaking until it finishes.
checks.question: What is the technical name for handling user interruptions, and what are the two things the system must cancel when a user interrupts?
options: A. Input validation; must cancel the HTTP request and clear the queue
B. Barge-in; must cancel the ongoing TTS playback and the in-progress LLM streaming call
C. Duplex audio; must close the WebSocket connection and reopen it
D. Interruption handling; must cancel only the TTS playback (the LLM can finish in the background)
checks.explanation: This is called barge-in - the ability for a user to 'barge into' the agent's speech. When barge-in is detected (via VAD detecting new user speech during TTS playback), two things must happen: (1) TTS playback must stop immediately so the user is not talking over the agent, and (2) the LLM streaming call must be cancelled to avoid wasting tokens completing a response that will be discarded. Letting the LLM finish in the background (option D) wastes compute and may cause confusion if the partial response is incorrectly used in the next turn.

checks.scenario: A voice agent team is choosing between claude-3-5-haiku-20241022 and claude-opus-4-5 for the LLM step in their pipeline. They run a quality benchmark and find that Opus produces measurably better responses for complex customer queries.
checks.question: Given a 500ms P95 latency target, why might they still choose Haiku over Opus?
options: A. Haiku is open-source while Opus requires a paid license
B. Opus does not support streaming output
C. Haiku's TTFT (time to first token) is 150-350ms vs Opus's 400-800ms, and higher TTFT directly causes first-audio latency to exceed the 500ms budget
D. Haiku supports more conversation turns per session than Opus
checks.explanation: LLM TTFT is one of the three main latency contributors in the voice pipeline. With STT taking 100ms and TTS taking 80ms, the latency budget for the LLM step is about 320ms if the total target is 500ms. Haiku's TTFT of 150-350ms fits within budget. Opus's TTFT of 400-800ms exceeds it, pushing total latency to 700-1100ms. The quality improvement may not be worth the latency cost for a real-time voice agent, even if Opus would be clearly better in an async text chat context.

checks.scenario: A team is building a voice agent for phone calls using Twilio. Twilio sends audio as mu-law encoded PCM at 8kHz. Deepgram expects PCM at 16kHz. The team tries to send Twilio audio directly to Deepgram and gets poor transcription quality.
checks.question: What preprocessing step is required and why?
options: A. Convert audio from stereo to mono - Deepgram only handles mono audio
B. Transcode from mu-law 8kHz to linear PCM 16kHz - Deepgram needs linear PCM and most STT models are trained on 16kHz audio
C. Base64-encode the audio before sending - Deepgram only accepts base64 audio
D. Reduce the audio bitrate - Deepgram has a maximum input bitrate limit
checks.explanation: Twilio uses mu-law encoding (a lossy compression format used in telephony) at 8kHz (phone bandwidth). Deepgram and most modern STT models expect linear PCM at 16kHz. Two conversions are needed: (1) mu-law to linear PCM (audioop.ulaw2lin in Python's stdlib), and (2) upsampling from 8kHz to 16kHz (audioop.ratecv or similar). Sending mu-law directly to Deepgram produces degraded transcription because the model interprets the compressed bytes as raw audio.

checks.scenario: A voice agent team wants to run regression tests on 200 conversation scenarios before each deployment. Running real audio through the full STT-LLM-TTS pipeline for 200 scenarios would take 30+ minutes and cost significant API fees.
checks.question: What testing approach lets them test the agent's reasoning quality in minutes without real audio?
options: A. Record all 200 conversations and play them back at 10x speed
B. Use synthetic text scenarios: inject text directly into the LLM step (bypassing STT), and compare LLM text output (bypassing TTS), testing only the reasoning logic
C. Only test the TTS quality since STT and LLM are from third-party providers
D. Use mocked LLM responses to avoid API calls entirely
checks.explanation: The agent's reasoning quality (what it says, whether it handles edge cases correctly, whether it triggers handoff appropriately) lives in the LLM step and is independent of STT/TTS. By injecting text directly and comparing text output, you can run 200 scenarios in under 2 minutes with minimal API cost (LLM calls only, no STT/TTS). This tests the intelligence of the agent. Separate tests cover STT accuracy and TTS quality. This separation is the right approach for efficient regression testing.

checks.scenario: A customer service voice agent has been live for 2 weeks. Analytics show: mean latency 420ms (within budget), turn completion rate 45% (agent is frequently interrupted), session completion rate 38% (most users request human handoff), handoff rate 35%.
checks.question: Which metric should the team prioritize improving first and why?
options: A. Latency - 420ms mean is too close to the 500ms target and needs more headroom
B. Turn completion rate - 45% means users are constantly interrupting the agent, suggesting responses are too long or off-topic before users get what they need
C. Session completion rate - improving this directly reduces support costs by resolving more issues without humans
D. Handoff rate - reducing it from 35% to 20% would be the quickest win
checks.explanation: The 45% turn completion rate is the most diagnostic signal here. Users interrupting the agent 55% of the time means the agent's responses are not landing: they are either too long (user loses patience), off-topic (user corrects the agent), or missing the point of the question. Fixing this will also improve session completion and reduce handoff rate. Latency at 420ms mean is within budget. Session completion (C) and handoff rate (D) are symptoms of the same underlying problem as turn completion rate.
